question: Merge vs rebase.
answer:
Merge combines branches with a merge commit — preserves the true history, non-destructive.

Rebase replays your commits on top of another branch — a linear, clean history, but rewrites commits. Never rebase branches others have pulled.
tip: Golden rule: don't rebase shared/public branches.
code: git rebase main   # replay feature commits onto main

question: git fetch vs git pull.
answer: fetch downloads remote changes but doesn't touch your working branch — you can inspect first. pull = fetch + merge (or rebase) into your current branch in one step.
tip: Use fetch + review before merging when you want to see what changed.

question: reset vs revert vs checkout.
answer:
• revert — creates a new commit that undoes a previous one; safe on shared branches.
  • reset — moves the branch pointer (soft/mixed/hard); rewrites history, use locally.
  • checkout / switch / restore — move between branches or restore files.
tip: On a shared branch, undo with revert (safe), not reset (rewrites history).
code:
git revert <hash>   # safe undo
git reset --hard HEAD~1  # local-only

question: What does git stash do?
answer: Temporarily shelves uncommitted changes so you can switch context (e.g. fix an urgent bug on another branch), then reapply them with git stash pop.
tip: git stash pop applies and drops; git stash apply keeps the stash.
code:
git stash
git switch main
# ... later ...
git switch feature && git stash pop

question: What is git cherry-pick?
answer: Applies a single specific commit from one branch onto another — useful for hotfixes you need on multiple branches without merging everything.
tip: Great for porting one bug-fix commit to a release branch.
code: git cherry-pick <hash>

question: Describe a common branching strategy.
answer: GitFlow — long-lived main and develop, plus feature/*, release/*, and hotfix/* branches. Simpler alternative: trunk-based / GitHub Flow — short-lived feature branches merged to main via pull requests.
tip: Most modern teams favour short-lived branches + PRs over full GitFlow.

question: How do you resolve a merge conflict?
answer: Git marks conflicting regions with <<<<<<< / ======= / >>>>>>>. Edit to the desired result, remove the markers, git add the files, then commit (or git rebase --continue).
tip: Pull/rebase frequently to keep conflicts small and manageable.

question: How do you recover a 'lost' commit?
answer: git reflog logs where HEAD has been, including commits orphaned by a reset/rebase. Find the hash and git checkout or git reset back to it.
tip: reflog is the safety net after a bad reset — commits aren't truly gone immediately.
code:
git reflog
git reset --hard <hash>

question: What is squashing and why do it?
answer: Combining several commits into one (via interactive rebase or a squash-merge) to keep the main branch history clean — one logical change = one commit.
tip: Squash-merge on PRs keeps main's history readable without losing branch detail.
code: git rebase -i HEAD~3   # mark commits as 'squash'

question: What is HEAD and a detached HEAD?
answer: HEAD points to your current commit/branch. A detached HEAD means you've checked out a specific commit rather than a branch — new commits there aren't on any branch and can be lost unless you create one.
tip: If you commit in detached HEAD, make a branch before switching away.
code: git switch -c new-branch  # save detached-HEAD work

question: What do you look for in a code review? Describe your PR process.
answer:
A layered checklist, most important first:

  • Correctness — does it do what the ticket says; edge cases (null/empty/concurrent); error handling.
  • Design — right layer for the logic, no duplication of existing utilities, sane API shapes, backward compatibility.
  • Safety — SQL injection/XSS, secrets in code, N+1 queries, missing indexes on new queries, transaction boundaries.
  • Tests — meaningful cases (not just coverage), failure paths tested.
  • Readability last — naming, dead code; style nits belong to the linter, not humans.

Process: small PRs (< ~400 lines review well), a description that says why, CI green before human eyes, comments phrased as questions, and approval means I'd maintain this.
tip: Have one story ready: a bug you caught in review (concurrency, missing rollback) and one piece of pushback you accepted gracefully — review questions are always secretly behavioral.

question: Working directory, staging area, and repository.
answer:
Git has three 'trees':

  • Working directory — the actual files you edit.
  • Staging area (index) — a snapshot you're preparing; git add moves changes here.
  • Repository — committed history; git commit records the staged snapshot permanently.

The staging area is what lets you commit only some of your changes.
tip: git add -p stages hunks selectively — the key to small, logical commits from a messy working tree.
code:
git add file.js      # working dir -> staging
git commit -m "msg"  # staging -> repository

question: clone vs fork.
answer:
clone — a Git operation that makes a local copy of a repository (with full history) that you can pull from and, with access, push to.

fork — a hosting-platform (GitHub/GitLab) concept: a server-side copy of a repo under your account. You fork a project you can't push to, clone your fork, then open a pull request back to the original.
tip: Fork is the open-source contribution model; clone is just getting the code locally. Fork lives on the server, clone on your machine.
code: git clone https://github.com/user/repo.git

question: .gitignore — how it works and the common gotcha.
answer:
.gitignore lists path patterns Git should not track (build output, node_modules, secrets). It only affects untracked files.

Gotcha: a file already tracked keeps being tracked even after you add it to .gitignore — you must untrack it explicitly.
tip: Committed a secret then added it to .gitignore? It's still in history — remove it with git rm --cached AND rotate the secret.
code:
git rm --cached .env      # stop tracking, keep the file
echo ".env" >> .gitignore

question: Inspecting history: log, diff, and show.
answer:
• git log — commit history; --oneline --graph for a compact branch view.
  • git diff — unstaged changes; --staged for what's about to commit; A..B to compare commits/branches.
  • git show <commit> — the full patch and metadata of one commit.
tip: git log --oneline --graph --all is the fastest way to see how your branches actually relate.
code:
git log --oneline --graph --all
git diff --staged
git show HEAD~2

question: Git tags — lightweight vs annotated.
answer:
A tag is a named pointer to a specific commit, typically marking a release.

  • Lightweight — just a name → commit pointer.
  • Annotated (-a) — a full object storing tagger, date, message, and can be signed. Recommended for releases.

Tags aren't pushed by default — you must push them explicitly.
tip: Use annotated tags for releases (they carry who/when/why); push with git push --tags or git push origin v1.0.
code:
git tag -a v1.2.0 -m "Release 1.2.0"
git push origin v1.2.0

question: What does git commit --amend do?
answer: It replaces the most recent commit with a new one — letting you fix the commit message or fold in additional staged changes you forgot. Technically it creates a brand-new commit (new hash) and moves the branch to it.
tip: Because it rewrites history, never amend a commit you've already pushed to a shared branch — you'd force others to reconcile.
code:
git add forgotten.js
git commit --amend --no-edit   # fold in, keep message

question: Interactive rebase — what can it do?
answer:
git rebase -i opens an editable list of commits so you can rewrite a series before merging:

  • reword — change a message
  • squash / fixup — combine commits (fixup discards the message)
  • edit — stop to amend a specific commit
  • reorder / drop — rearrange or delete commits
tip: Clean up your messy WIP commits into a tidy story right before opening the PR — but only on your own un-pushed branch.
code: git rebase -i HEAD~4   # curate the last 4 commits

question: Fast-forward vs no-ff merge.
answer:
A fast-forward merge happens when the target branch hasn't diverged — Git just moves the branch pointer forward, no merge commit, a perfectly linear history.

--no-ff forces a merge commit even when a fast-forward is possible, preserving the fact that a feature branch existed as a distinct group of commits.
tip: Many teams merge PRs with --no-ff so each feature is one revertable merge commit in main's history.
code: git merge --no-ff feature   # always create a merge commit

question: git pull --rebase vs a plain pull.
answer:
A default git pull does fetch + merge, which can litter history with 'Merge branch main' commits when you and the remote both moved.

git pull --rebase instead replays your local commits on top of the fetched remote — a clean, linear history with no extra merge commits.
tip: Set it once and forget it: git config --global pull.rebase true.
code: git pull --rebase origin main

question: How does git bisect find a bug?
answer: A binary search through history for the commit that introduced a bug. You mark a known-bad commit and a known-good one; Git checks out the midpoint, you test and mark it good/bad, and it halves the range each step until it pinpoints the culprit.
tip: Automate it: git bisect run ./test.sh lets Git find the breaking commit hands-free across hundreds of commits.
code:
git bisect start
git bisect bad            # current is broken
git bisect good v1.0     # this tag worked
# test each checkout, then:  git bisect good|bad
git bisect reset

question: What is git blame for?
answer: git blame annotates each line of a file with the commit, author, and date that last changed it — the way to find when and why a specific line came to be, then read that commit's message/PR for context.
tip: Blame tells you the last change, not the origin — use git log -L to follow a line's full evolution.
code: git blame -L 40,60 src/app.js   # who touched lines 40-60

question: What are Git hooks?
answer:
Scripts Git runs automatically on events: pre-commit (lint/format), commit-msg (enforce message format), pre-push (run tests), post-merge, etc.

They live in .git/hooks and are not committed — so teams use tools like Husky or pre-commit to share and version them.
tip: Hooks are local-only by design; to guarantee a check for everyone, enforce it in CI too — a dev can always skip a hook with --no-verify.
code:
# .husky/pre-commit
npm run lint && npm test

question: reset --soft vs --mixed vs --hard.
answer:
All three move HEAD to a target commit; they differ in what else they touch:

  • --soft — moves HEAD only; changes stay staged.
  • --mixed (default) — moves HEAD and unstages; changes remain in the working directory.
  • --hard — moves HEAD and discards both staged and working changes.
tip: --soft is the 'uncommit but keep everything' undo; --hard is destructive — recover only via reflog if you regret it.
code:
git reset --soft HEAD~1   # undo last commit, keep changes staged
git reset --hard HEAD~1   # undo last commit, throw changes away

question: What are Git submodules?
answer:
A submodule embeds one repository inside another, pinned to a specific commit of the child. The parent tracks only that pointer, not the child's files.

Pros: share a library across projects with exact version control. Cons: extra ceremony — clones need --recurse-submodules, and it's easy to forget to commit an updated pointer.
tip: Fresh clone missing submodule files? Run git submodule update --init --recursive.
code:
git clone --recurse-submodules <url>
git submodule update --init --recursive

question: How do you remove a secret or large file from all of history?
answer:
Deleting it in a new commit isn't enough — it's still in every prior commit. Use git filter-repo (the modern replacement for filter-branch, or the BFG tool) to rewrite every commit and purge the file/string.

This changes all commit hashes, so you must force-push and everyone must re-clone — and you should rotate the leaked secret regardless, since it may already be cloned.
tip: Rewriting history is the cleanup; rotating the credential is the actual fix — assume any pushed secret is already compromised.
code:
git filter-repo --path secrets.env --invert-paths
git push --force --all

question: What is git worktree?
answer: git worktree checks out multiple branches into separate directories backed by a single repository (shared .git). You can work on an urgent hotfix in one folder without stashing or disturbing your half-done feature in another.
tip: Worktrees beat stashing when you need to build/run two branches at once — each has its own working files and index.
code:
git worktree add ../hotfix main
# fix, commit, then:
git worktree remove ../hotfix

question: Explain Git's object model.
answer:
Git is a content-addressable store of four object types, each keyed by a hash of its content:

  • blob — file contents.
  • tree — a directory listing (names → blobs/trees).
  • commit — a snapshot: root tree + parent commit(s) + author/message.
  • tag — an annotated pointer to a commit.

Refs (branches) are just movable pointers to commit hashes.
tip: The killer insight: a branch is merely a pointer to a commit — that's why creating/switching branches is instant and cheap.
code:
git cat-file -t HEAD    # commit
git cat-file -p HEAD    # inspect the object graph
